skill.name: wp-playground
skill.description: Guides WordPress Playground workflows for disposable WordPress instances, local CLI servers, blueprints, snapshots, version testing, and Xdebug debugging.
skill.summary: Run and debug disposable WordPress Playground environments with CLI, blueprints, snapshots, and version controls.
skill.author: Automattic

security_audit.summary: Static command and weak-cryptography alerts were mostly markdown false positives, with no prompt injection or malicious intent found. The skill does provide legitimate instructions for npx CLI execution, remote blueprint resources, local file mounting, PHP execution, and file-writing blueprint steps, so publication should include a medium-risk warning.

medium_findings.title: Legitimate CLI Command Execution Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to run npx @wp-playground/cli commands. This is expected for the workflow, but users should review project paths, package versions, and blueprint inputs before execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are plainly shown in markdown as user-run CLI examples, not hidden automation. Risk remains because npx @latest and user-supplied paths or blueprints can execute external tooling.

medium_findings.title: Blueprints Can Run PHP And Modify Files
medium_findings.description: The blueprint reference documents runPHP, applyPatches, writeFile, installPlugin, installTheme, and adjacent file access. This is normal for WordPress setup, but untrusted blueprints can modify a Playground instance or fetch remote packages.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced blueprint steps directly include PHP execution and filesystem modification capabilities. The context is legitimate documentation, so this is a controlled workflow risk rather than malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Local File Mounting Requires Secret Hygiene
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends auto-mounting or explicit host path mounts into Playground. This can expose local plugin, theme, or site files to the test instance if users mount broad directories.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file mounting behavior is explicit and expected. The risk depends on user-selected paths and whether secrets are present in mounted project files.

low_findings.title: Official URL References Are Low Risk
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to WordPress Playground schema, wordpress.org downloads, localhost, and upstream documentation. No evidence of data exfiltration or suspicious third-party endpoints was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation or official WordPress resources. I did not find evidence of credential collection, tracking endpoints, or covert network use.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static weak-cryptography flags land on prose, metadata, or headings, not cryptographic code. No hashing, encryption, or credential handling implementation appears in the scanned skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain ordinary markdown text about WordPress Playground. There is no code path using MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or similar weak algorithms.

dangerous_patterns.description: The skill instructs users to run npx @wp-playground/cli commands. This is expected for the workflow, but users should review project paths, package versions, and blueprint inputs before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are plainly shown in markdown as user-run CLI examples, not hidden automation. Risk remains because npx @latest and user-supplied paths or blueprints can execute external tooling.

dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The referenced blueprint steps directly include PHP execution and filesystem modification capabilities. The context is legitimate documentation, so this is a controlled workflow risk rather than malicious behavior.

dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file mounting behavior is explicit and expected. The risk depends on user-selected paths and whether secrets are present in mounted project files.

content.value_statement: WordPress testing often needs fast, isolated sites without Docker or a database. This skill guides Playground CLI, blueprints, snapshots, versions, mounts, and debugging.
actual_capabilities: Start local WordPress Playground servers with auto-mounted plugin or theme projects.
Run Playground blueprints from local files or approved URLs.
Build shareable Playground snapshots for bug reports or review workflows.
Switch WordPress and PHP versions for compatibility testing.
Set up Xdebug-oriented debugging steps for mounted plugin or theme code.
Troubleshoot mounts, ports, blueprint assets, and slow Playground runs.
limitations: It does not replace a production WordPress stack or full database environment.
It requires Node.js, npm or npx, and a compatible Playground CLI release.
It cannot guarantee untrusted blueprints or remote ZIP files are safe.
Some PHP extensions, native databases, and hosting-specific behavior may not match production.

use_cases.title: Test Plugin Changes Locally
use_cases.description: Mount a plugin directory into a disposable Playground site and verify activation, UI paths, and version compatibility.

use_cases.title: Share Reproducible Bug Reports
use_cases.description: Create a blueprint or snapshot that reproduces a site state for reviewers without a full local stack.

use_cases.title: Debug Theme Behavior
use_cases.description: Run a mounted theme with Xdebug guidance and targeted troubleshooting for paths, ports, and slow runs.

prompt_templates.title: Start A Local Playground
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a plugin or theme folder.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me start WordPress Playground for this plugin or theme. Check prerequisites, choose safe defaults, and explain each command before running it.

prompt_templates.title: Create A Test Blueprint
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a WordPress Playground blueprint for my test scenario. Include required steps, local files, version choices, and safety notes for remote resources.

prompt_templates.title: Build A Shareable Snapshot
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide me through building a Playground snapshot from my blueprint. Validate the setup, choose an output name, and list verification steps.

prompt_templates.title: Debug A Mounted Project
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced troubleshooting with Xdebug and CLI flags.
prompt_templates.prompt: Troubleshoot my WordPress Playground run with Xdebug. Review mounts, port conflicts, version flags, blueprint access, and breakpoint path mappings.

output_examples.input: I need to test a plugin quickly in WordPress Playground.
output: A prerequisite checklist for Node.js and npx.
A local server plan using the plugin folder as the mount target.
Verification steps for plugin activation and a smoke test.

output_examples.input: I want to share a reproducible WordPress bug with another developer.
output: A blueprint-first workflow for the required theme, plugin, and version state.
A snapshot creation plan with a clear output file name.
A short checklist for confirming the snapshot opens correctly.

output_examples.input: Breakpoints do not work in my Playground debugging session.
output: A focused checklist for Xdebug startup flags, IDE listener settings, and path mappings.
A mount verification step to confirm source paths match the Playground filesystem.
Fallback steps for collecting debug logs from the CLI output.

best_practices: Review every blueprint and remote ZIP URL before running it locally.
Mount the smallest project directory needed, and remove secrets before testing.
Pin WordPress, PHP, and CLI versions when reproducing bugs or sharing snapshots.
anti_patterns: Do not run untrusted blueprints with PHP execution or file-writing steps.
Do not mount production directories, secrets, uploads, or database exports into Playground.
Do not rely on ephemeral Playground state as a permanent development environment.

faq.question: Does this skill install WordPress Playground automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides workflow guidance and commands. You still control whether commands run in your environment.

faq.question: Can I use it without Docker?
faq.answer: Yes. WordPress Playground runs WordPress through WebAssembly and SQLite, using the browser or the Playground CLI.

faq.question: Can it test plugins and themes?
faq.answer: Yes. It supports auto-mounting or explicit mounts for plugin and theme directories during local Playground runs.

faq.question: Are remote blueprints safe?
faq.answer: Only use trusted blueprints. They can install packages, run PHP, and write files inside the Playground environment.

faq.question: Can it reproduce production hosting behavior?
faq.answer: Not completely. Playground is useful for isolated tests, but some server, database, and extension behavior may differ.

faq.question: Does it support debugging?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes Xdebug-oriented guidance for CLI flags, IDE listener setup, path mappings, and troubleshooting.
